text: Hey everybody, today I'm presenting a robotic sigmoid colectomy for recurrent diverticulitis in a female patient that has had a prior hysterectomy and also recently had a failed colonoscopy, actually a couple of attempts because they could not navigate the sigmoid colon. But aside from that, I'm going to share with you that my wife had a baby yesterday, so that is super exciting, our second girl. And a little added bonus, she looks like me, so no concern there.

I've been doing more and more of these cases with a lateral to medial just to get the sigmoid colon on a nice long mesentery because I'm not routinely anymore doing high ligations if I have enough length.

The surgical setup includes four ports for natural orifice extraction: a varus needle at Palmer's point in the left subcostal area, a 12mm trocar at McBurney's point in the right lower quadrant, and a 5mm assistant port in the right flank. The patient is positioned in a steep 25-degree Trendelenburg, leaning to the right.

The surgeon encounters extensive adhesions in the pelvis, likely due to diverticular disease and previous hysterectomy. The redundant sigmoid colon is visibly folded over itself in the pelvic region. After addressing the adhesions, the sigmoid colon is straightened out, facilitating the continuation of the procedure.

The surgeon removes the last bits of scar tissue, revealing the redundant sigmoid colon folded on itself. The colon is pulled up out of the pelvis, and the final band adhesion is released. The proximal resection margin is defined, showing a healthy colon with minimal diverticular disease.

The surgeon creates a mesenteric window at the proximal resection margin, carefully dissecting down the mesentery while preserving the superior rectal artery. The mesentery appears favorable, allowing the vessel sealer to effectively compress the tissue and achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon approaches the rectal mesentery, preparing to extend the dissection to the distal resection margin.

The surgeon administers 3 milliliters of ICG intravenously to assess perfusion, confirming good proximal and distal resection margins. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon performs a natural orifice extraction, initially cutting at the distal resection margin. Observing the narrowness and presence of epiploica, the surgeon uses a rigid proctoscope and a 25mm balsizer to identify the sigmoid colon instead of the recto-sigmoid junction. The surgeon then excises an additional 4-5 centimeters of sigmoid colon using the vessel sealer, reaching the rectum for a better distal resection margin.
The surgeon further frees up adhesions in the pelvic region, straightening out the redundant sigmoid colon in preparation for the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a 29mm EEA anvil into the proximal sigmoid colon by creating a small collotomy in the specimen portion, allowing the anvil to pass through. The collotomy is made just large enough to fit the anvil, demonstrating a method of anvil placement for anastomosis.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to ensure proper perfusion of the tissues. The procedure involves performing anastomoses and transanal specimen removal, with the surgeon alternating between robotic and manual techniques. The process is meticulous but avoids the need for a larger incision and the use of a wound protector.

The surgeon separates the sigmoid colon mesentery from the colon, tubularizing the colon for easier transanal extraction. The colon appears less thick and woody compared to other cases, facilitating a smoother removal process.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the integrity of the connection. The test shows no signs of leakage.
